    "notes": {
      "notes": {
        "9ff30b00-c0d6-4f4a-8ee1-8f810d5deb46": {
          "id": "9ff30b00-c0d6-4f4a-8ee1-8f810d5deb46",
          "user_id": "4cf62e4e-2679-484f-b652-034e697418df",
          "label": "War Room note",
          "content": "# Scope → Agent Binding — Design\n\n**Status:** Proposal (not yet built). Frontend will be built against the contract defined here.\n**Author:** design doc, 2026-06-13\n**Related:** [`deferred-context-system.md`](deferred-context-system.md) · `aidream/api/core/agent_run.py` · `aidream/api/utils/context_utils.py` · `aidream/api/context_objects.py` · `packages/matrx-ai/matrx_ai/context_engine.py`\n\n---\n\n## 1. The problem in one sentence\n\nWhen we call an agent, there is **no deterministic mechanism that takes the context items stored on a scope and uses them to fill the agent's variables or the agent's context slots.** Scope data and agent inputs are two separate pipelines that never meet.\n\nThis doc proposes the missing primitive: an **explicit, ID-based binding** declared on the agent that says *\"fill this variable / this context slot from this scope context item\"*, resolved server-side at agent-call time.\n\n---\n\n## 2. What exists today (verified against code)\n\nThere are two completely separate pipelines. Neither reads scope items into agent inputs.\n\n### Pipeline A — Agent variables (`{{substitution}}`)\n\n- Source: `request.variables` + `ide_state` only (client-supplied).\n- `prepare_agent_run` builds `initial_variables`, resolves picklists, then `config.replace_variables(...)`.\n- Agent definition carries `variable_defaults: dict[str, AgentVariable]` (name, default_value, required, custom_component).\n- **Scope is never consulted to fill a variable.**\n\n### Pipeline B — Agent context slots (deferred context objects)\n\n- Source: `request.context` dict only (client-supplied).\n- `ContextManifest.build(agent_slots, request_context)` matches by **string key**: `slot_map.get(key)` where `key` comes from the *client's* `context` dict (`aidream/api/context_objects.py`).\n- Agent definition carries `context_slots: list[ContextSlot]` (`prompts.context_slots` JSONB). `ContextSlot` already has an unused-for-this `source: ContextSource` field.\n- **Scope is never consulted to fill a slot.**\n\n### Pipeline C — Scope context (system-prompt awareness only)\n\n- `resolve_agent_context_block` → `build_agent_context` → `resolve_full_context` RPC.\n- Returns scope IDs, `scope_labels`, and `variables` (split by `inject_as` into direct/tool/searchable).\n- Rendered into an `<agent_context>` block injected into the **system prompt** (`AgentContext.build_system_prompt_block`).\n- This is read-only awareness text. It does **not** flow into variable substitution or into context slots.\n\n**Conclusion:** scope context items reach the model only as flavor text in the system prompt. They never deterministically populate a named variable or a named context slot.\n\n---\n\n## 3. The scope-side data model (the source of truth we bind FROM)\n\n```\nctx_scope_types          one row per \"kind of scope\" (Client, Department, Project…) per org\n  ├─ id (uuid)           ← stable identifier we bind against\n  ├─ slug / label_*      human labels\n  └─ default_variable_keys  text[]  (item keys this type exposes as variables by default)\n\nctx_context_items        DEFINITION of an item, attached to a scope_type\n  ├─ id (uuid)           ← stable identifier we bind against\n  ├─ key (text)          ← stable identifier we bind against (e.g. \"brand_voice\")\n  ├─ scope_type_id       which type this item belongs to\n  ├─ value_type (enum)   string | number | boolean | json | document | reference | date\n  ├─ display_name, description, sensitivity, is_active\n  └─ ...\n\nctx_scopes               INSTANCE of a scope (e.g. \"Acme Corp\" of type Client)\n  ├─ id (uuid)\n  ├─ scope_type_id\n  ├─ organization_id, parent_scope_id (hierarchy)\n\nctx_context_item_values  the actual VALUE of an item for a specific scope instance\n  ├─ scope_id            which scope instance\n  ├─ context_item_id     which item definition\n  ├─ is_current (bool)   only one current version per (scope_id, item)\n  ├─ version (int)\n  └─ value_text | value_number | value_boolean | value_json | value_document_url\n     | value_date | value_reference_id   (typed columns, picked by item.value_type)\n```\n\nPlus a parallel, simpler store: `ctx_context_variables` (`key`, `value` jsonb, `value_type`, `inject_as`, scoped by user/org/project/task columns). This is what the `resolve_full_context` RPC's `variables` payload comes from today.\n\n**Two source shapes, one binding mechanism.** The binding declared on the agent should be able to target EITHER a structured `ctx_context_items`/value OR a `ctx_context_variables` row. The resolver normalizes both to a concrete value.\n\n---\n\n## 4. Core principle: bind by stable identity, never by name coincidence\n\nThe thing we are NOT going to do (and the reason this doc exists): silently matching a scope item's `key` to an agent variable/slot of the same name. That is fragile, surprising, and breaks the moment someone renames either side.\n\nA binding is an **explicit, authored declaration on the agent definition** that points at a scope item by stable identity. Resolution is pure lookup — no fuzzy matching, no heuristics.\n\nA binding always carries enough to resolve deterministically:\n\n| Field | Meaning |\n|-------|---------|\n| `source` | `\"ctx_item\"` (structured item) or `\"ctx_variable\"` (simple variable row) |\n| `scope_type_id` | which TYPE of scope the value lives on (e.g. the \"Client\" type) |\n| `item_key` **or** `item_id` | which item on that type (key is portable across orgs; id is exact) |\n| `target` | `\"variable\"` or `\"context_slot\"` |\n| `target_key` | the agent variable name / context-slot key to fill |\n| `on_missing` | `\"empty\"` (default) \\| `\"skip\"` \\| `\"error\"` |\n| `scope_selector` | how to choose among multiple active scopes of that type (see §6) |\n\nBinding by `scope_type_id` + `item_key` (not a hard `scope_id`) is what makes one agent reusable across many scope instances: the active scope set for *this* request decides which concrete `ctx_scopes` row supplies the value.\n\n---\n\n## 5. Where the bindings live\n\nTwo options; recommendation is **A**.\n\n**A. Bindings declared on the agent definition (recommended).**\nExtend the existing per-target structures so an author wires this once:\n\n- `AgentVariable` (matrx-ai `agents/variables.py`, already `extra=\"allow\"`) gains an optional `binding` field.\n- `ContextSlot` (`aidream/api/context_objects.py`) already has `source: ContextSource`. Extend `ContextSource.kind` to accept `\"ctx_item\"` / `\"ctx_variable\"` and add `scope_type_id` / `item_key` so a slot can declare its scope source directly.\n\nThis keeps the binding next to the thing it fills, travels with the agent, and is visible in the agent editor UI we're about to build.\n\n**B. A separate `agx_scope_bindings` table** keyed by agent_id.\nMore flexible for bulk editing but splits the agent's input contract across two places. Only choose this if bindings need to be managed independently of the agent definition (not the case today).\n\n---\n\n## 6. Resolving \"which scope instance\" deterministically (the hard part)\n\nA binding names a scope *type*. At request time there may be zero, one, or many active scopes of that type. The active set is what `resolve_full_context` already computes: the union of `request.scope_ids` (client global picker) and the entity's `ctx_scope_assignments`, membership-validated against the user.\n\nDeterministic selection rule, in order:\n\n1. **Explicit scope id on the binding** — if the binding pins `scope_id`, use it (after validating it's in the active set). Highest precedence, fully deterministic.\n2. **Exactly one active scope of the type** — use it.\n3. **Multiple active scopes of the type** — apply `scope_selector`:\n   - `\"nearest\"` (default): walk the scope hierarchy from the entity's most specific assignment upward (`parent_scope_id` chain) and take the first match of the type.\n   - `\"explicit_only\"`: do not guess — treat as missing (apply `on_missing`).\n   - `\"first_by_sort\"`: deterministic fallback ordering by `(sort_order, created_at, id)`.\n4. **Zero active scopes of the type** — missing; apply `on_missing`.\n\nThe selection must be a pure function of `(active scope set, binding)` — same inputs always pick the same scope. No \"most recently used\", no time-based heuristics.\n\nOnce a `scope_id` is chosen, the value is the single `ctx_context_item_values` row where `scope_id = chosen AND context_item_id = resolved item AND is_current = true`. (`is_current` already guarantees one row.)\n\n---\n\n## 7. Value coercion (`value_type` → target)\n\nThe resolver reads the correct typed column and coerces to what the target expects:\n\n| `value_type` | Source column | → variable (string substitution) | → context slot (`ContextObjectType`) |\n|--------------|---------------|-----------------------------------|--------------------------------------|\n| `string` | `value_text` | as-is | `text` |\n| `number` | `value_number` | `str(n)` | `text` (or `json`) |\n| `boolean` | `value_boolean` | `\"true\"/\"false\"` | `text` |\n| `json` | `value_json` | `json.dumps(...)` | `json` |\n| `date` | `value_date` | ISO string | `text` |\n| `document` | `value_document_url` | the URL string | `file_url` |\n| `reference` | `value_reference_id` (+ type) | resolved label/id | `db_ref` / `text` |\n\nVariables are always strings (substitution contract). Context slots keep the richer type so the manifest renders/handles them correctly.\n\n---\n\n## 8. Injection points (deterministic, no new model surface)\n\nThe whole feature is ONE new resolver called from `prepare_agent_run`, feeding the two existing pipelines. No changes to substitution or to `ContextManifest.build` semantics.\n\n```\nprepare_agent_run:\n  ...\n  initial_variables = {**ide, **request.variables}     # existing\n\n  # NEW: resolve scope bindings into a normalized result\n  scope_bound = await resolve_scope_bindings(\n      agent_config,                    # carries variable + slot bindings\n      active_scope_ids=request.scope_ids,\n      entity_type=\"conversation\",\n      entity_id=conversation_id,\n      user_id=ctx.user_id,\n  )\n  # scope_bound.variables : dict[str, str]      (target_key -> coerced string)\n  # scope_bound.context   : dict[str, Any]      (slot_key   -> coerced content)\n\n  # Merge into variable pipeline with correct PRECEDENCE (see §9)\n  initial_variables = {**scope_bound.variables, **initial_variables}\n\n  # Merge into context pipeline: synthesize request.context entries so the\n  # EXISTING ContextManifest.build fills the slots — no string coincidence,\n  # the key is the binding's target_key, authored by the agent.\n  merged_context = {**scope_bound.context, **(request.context or {})}\n  ...\n  apply_context_objects(config, ctx, merged_context, agent_config.context_slots, ...)\n```\n\nKey properties:\n\n- Reuses `resolve_full_context` for the active-scope membership/validation it already does (don't re-implement ACL).\n- Variables flow through the **existing** `replace_variables` path.\n- Slots flow through the **existing** `ContextManifest.build` path — the synthesized `merged_context[target_key]` now matches the agent's slot key *because the agent authored both ends*, not because two unrelated names happened to collide.\n- One call site. If you deleted it, the feature is gone and nothing else breaks.\n\n---\n\n## 9. Precedence (must be explicit and total)\n\nFor a given variable name, lowest → highest:\n\n1. Agent `default_value` (from `variable_defaults`)\n2. **Scope-bound value** (this feature)\n3. Explicit client value (`request.variables` / `ide_state`)\n\nRationale: an explicit per-request value from the caller is the strongest intent; scope provides the org-level default; the agent default is the floor. Same ordering for context slots: a client-supplied `request.context[key]` overrides the scope-bound fill (hence `{**scope_bound.context, **request.context}`).\n\nThis must be documented on the wire contract so the frontend can show the author \"this slot is filled from scope unless the caller overrides it.\"\n\n---\n\n## 10. Missing values — fail loud per policy, never silently wrong\n\nPer `on_missing`:\n\n- `\"empty\"` (default): variable → `\"\"`, slot → skipped (matches current \"slot with no content is skipped\" behavior). Record a structured trace entry so it's observable.\n- `\"skip\"`: same as empty but explicitly intended (no trace noise).\n- `\"error\"`: raise a typed, self-identifying error (`scope_binding_unresolved`) naming the binding, the scope type, and the active scope set. Use for bindings the agent genuinely cannot run without.\n\nNever substitute a wrong scope's value to avoid an empty — an ambiguous multi-scope case resolves via §6 or becomes missing, never a guess.\n\n---\n\n## 11. Observability\n\nEvery resolution emits a structured record (reuse the tool-trace/`vcprint` conventions): binding id, target, chosen `scope_id` + how it was chosen (rule 1–4), `context_item_id`, `value_type`, resolved length/hash (not the value if `sensitivity` is high or `is_secret`), and `on_missing` outcome. This makes \"why did the agent get THAT value\" a one-grep answer.\n\nSecrets (`ctx_context_variables.is_secret`, high `sensitivity` items) follow the existing picklist/secret pattern: never persisted into message history, staged only into the provider send-clone if ever needed.\n\n---\n\n## 12. Frontend contract (what the agent editor builds against)\n\nPer agent, the editor needs:\n\n1. **List the active scope types** available to the org (`ctx_scope_types`).\n2. For a chosen type, **list its items** (`ctx_context_items` where `scope_type_id = …`, `is_active = true`) with `key`, `display_name`, `value_type`, `description`.\n3. **Create a binding** by picking: source item → target (variable or context slot) → `target_key` → `scope_selector` → `on_missing`.\n4. For **variables**: the binding attaches to an existing `AgentVariable.binding`. For **context slots**: it attaches to a `ContextSlot.source`.\n5. **Preview**: given a concrete active scope, show the resolved value (calls a read-only resolve endpoint mirroring §6–§7) so the author sees exactly what the agent will receive.\n\nSuggested binding wire shape (single shape for both targets):\n\n```jsonc\n{\n  \"source\": \"ctx_item\",            // | \"ctx_variable\"\n  \"scope_type_id\": \"<uuid>\",\n  \"item_key\": \"brand_voice\",       // or \"item_id\": \"<uuid>\"\n  \"target\": \"context_slot\",        // | \"variable\"\n  \"target_key\": \"brand_voice\",     // agent slot key / variable name\n  \"scope_selector\": \"nearest\",     // | \"explicit_only\" | \"first_by_sort\"\n  \"scope_id\": null,                // optional hard pin\n  \"on_missing\": \"empty\"            // | \"skip\" | \"error\"\n}\n```\n\n---\n\n## 13. Build order\n\n1. **Backend resolver** `resolve_scope_bindings(...)` (pure, deterministic, §6–§7) + its read-only `preview` sibling. Unit-test selection rules with multi-scope fixtures.\n2. **Schema/contract**: add `binding` to `AgentVariable`; extend `ContextSource` for scope sources. Persist on `prompts`/`agx_agent` definition JSONB.\n3. **Wire into `prepare_agent_run`** at the single call site (§8) with precedence (§9) and observability (§11).\n4. **Frontend** agent-editor binding UI + preview (§12).\n5. **Docs**: promote the verified behavior into a `FEATURE.md` beside the resolver once it's live.\n\n---\n\n## 14. Acceptance tests\n\n- *Determinism:* identical `(agent, active scope set)` → byte-identical resolved variables + context every run.\n- *No name coincidence:* a scope item whose key equals an agent variable name but has **no binding** does NOT fill that variable.\n- *Multi-scope:* two active scopes of the same type resolve via the documented selector, or become missing under `explicit_only` — never a silent pick.\n- *Precedence:* explicit client value beats scope-bound value beats agent default, for both variables and slots.\n- *Missing policy:* `error` bindings raise a typed, self-identifying error; `empty`/`skip` behave as specified and are traced.\n",
          "folder_name": "Draft",

        "76641c35-2fae-46f4-8fcf-60032f536747": {
          "id": "76641c35-2fae-46f4-8fcf-60032f536747",

          "description": "This is where you don't leave current thread but start a new one with a very quick:\n\n1) Click\n2) Start typing name (focus on right input)\n3) Enter to save, tab to enter description\n4) Tab and enter to save, or click an option to open it and navigate to it. ",

        "f424e94d-ceda-435b-9bea-95bfd27d357a": {
          "id": "f424e94d-ceda-435b-9bea-95bfd27d357a",
          "title": "Re-initialize Tool components",
          "status": "incomplete",
          "priority": null,
          "due_date": null,
          "assignee_id": null,
          "project_id": null,
          "parent_task_id": null,
          "organization_id": "3e790542-fdaf-40b2-8bf3-658bf94fe67f",
          "description": null,

        "71e5067f-e444-4092-8f93-53df82f033d8": {
          "id": "71e5067f-e444-4092-8f93-53df82f033d8",
          "title": "Ensure an agent call gets the truth stored at each user message for context, tools, etc.",
          "status": "incomplete",
          "priority": null,
          "due_date": null,
          "assignee_id": null,
          "project_id": null,
          "parent_task_id": null,
          "organization_id": "3e790542-fdaf-40b2-8bf3-658bf94fe67f",
          "description": "Right now when a message is sent, we're saving either partial data or even incorrect data, so it's impossible to know exactly what was included in a call. And this is especially important for the things that are attached that the client side needs to show. So the user, the client can actually show exactly what resources were attached to some thing, what context was available at that message, and all of those things. And right now it cannot. And the database doesn't properly persisted either, so you can't see. And the other things you can't see is tools. For each call, we need to be recording what tools were attached. And right now, we're not.",
          "created_at": "2026-06-20T16:55:44.76295+00:00",
          "user_id": "4cf62e4e-2679-484f-b652-034e697418df"
        },
        "bbefcd41-492d-4692-b15f-212d21cf34c4": {
          "id": "bbefcd41-492d-4692-b15f-212d21cf34c4",
          "title": "Create Full Subagent system is AI Dream",
          "status": "incomplete",
          "priority": null,
          "due_date": null,
          "assignee_id": null,
          "project_id": null,
          "parent_task_id": null,
          "organization_id": "3e790542-fdaf-40b2-8bf3-658bf94fe67f",
          "description": "Initial documents here:\n\n/Users/armanisadeghi/code/aidream/packages/matrx-ai/matrx_ai/docs/SUBAGENTS.md\n\nI like the recommended list you're giving me, as long as you can ensure that all of the options we've discussed are listed and considered so that we can make sure to get them all planned out and completed. \n\nA few key things:\n1. In order to really test these our properly, we have to have a way to trigger a path that essentially 'fakes' the subagent calls so there is no cost to it but still follows THE EXACT path a normal call would take so we see all of the potential side effects. What I\"m thinking we do is to create a new provider that is exactly like the others, but it never makes an actual api call and then make the settings such that we can create different behaviors, including the time for the response, possibly making internal tool calls and also controlling the actual response or type of response. This is important for testing or it will become next to impossible to do this, but it has to be as real as it gets, including adding an actual model entry so as far as the system is concerned, it's all the same, but when it comes time for the thing to call and get a respnose, it calls an internal utility instead. This will allow us to test out even calling 10+ agents at once and fanning them out indefinitly without ever worrying about the cost or crazy things happening.\n\n2. We have to consider that with all of these options, we also have to consider ctx (context) and parent/child relationships for conversations. The key is that we will sometimes want the agents to share context and other times, we'll want separate context, etc. These are important options.\n\n3. A few of our systems are going to be challenged by these concepts so they need to be checked and possibly hardened before we do this.\n\nAt a minimum, I can already see potential weaknesses or The NEED FOR NEW RULES in these:\n\nCTX Context:\n- Handling context sharing, transition, new, etc. for one or multiple agents together\n- Rules regarding how to handle things such as \"user request id\" and other values that can get confusing if you don't pay attention. It's important to remember that a user request (cx_user_request) is a single api request that comes in. Doesn't reset unless another one from the user comes in. Everything lives inside of it. Conversation (cx_conversation), however ,is a single conversation back and forth with an agent. There could be 100 turns in a single user request or just one user message and one agent response. Provider API Request (cx_request) is a SINGLE request using the provider's api. Message sent and a response. that's it.\n\nORM Persistence:\n- Our orm has a system where it helps speed things along by accepting things it stores in cache and then writes later so the app can fire and forget without risking dataloss and other things. \n- That means it then needs to make sure that it keeps things in order so db references are there when needed. \n- Even in our current system that is no where near as complex, this system is consistently a problem because of either poor usage or weaknesses in the code. If CTX and this are not properly handled and we don't properly manage things like user_request.id, parent_conversation.id and other things like that, the entire thing will become a gigantic disaster and fail.\n\nStreaming Response: \n- We need to consider how responses will be streamed back to the frontend.\n- part of it is looking at what we already have and what we don't have.\n- For example, we may need to issue a new id and dispatch a signal to the FE to give them the new one so they can listen to both (or all) -- I think we already do this but you need to check.\n- It's critical that we stream everything back in real time from all sources, but at the same time, we need to make sure the FE fully understands what is for what so the UI doesn't start to get messed up.\n\nDatabase storage, sources and things like that:\n- It's very important that all of the data, categories, and other fields are set deliberately. For example, we track the app and feature that calls so we'll need to determine what those will be here. We could pass the same values through or we could create a new value.\n- Somehow, we need to ensure we capture that it's a subagent situation so the ui handles it correctly, but the details have to be thought out AND MOST IMPORTANTLY, discussed with the frontend so we come to a full solution that both sides agree on and are fully aware of. \n\nTo start, let's make sure we get these things all sorted out and document as much as possible so we can then figure out implementations.\n\nI want you to create a task list for yourself and document things so you don't forget anything. The first goal is planning and documentation, not full implementation. (Except for the testing api thing we discussed)\n\nIf you have questions for me, ask here in the chat (not the question tool) so that we can actually have a conversation and figure things out.\n\nKeep a solid todo list, document the things say clearly so we don't forget and if we can get things done that are easy \"ADDITIONS\" to code that we know won't break anything, just do them. No problem with adding things that are purely additive and non-destructive as we go. \n\n Feel free to use subagents for analysis but once they give you facts, go confirm the code yourself. It's very important to do that.\n\nAlso, when you create documentation and things like that, get adversarial agents to go through it and give you feedback, find holes, problems, etc. ",
          "created_at": "2026-06-21T15:08:25.483317+00:00",
          "user_id": "4cf62e4e-2679-484f-b652-034e697418df"
        },
